name: legendkit

metadata.name: legendkit
metadata.summary: Legend creation and manipulation with ease for matplotlib

documentation: [![Documentation Status](https://img.shields.io/readthedocs/legendkit?logo=readthedocs&logoColor=white&style=flat-square)](https://legendkit.readthedocs.io/en/stable) ![pypi version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/legendkit?color=blue&logo=python&logoColor=white&style=flat-square) When you want to create or adjust the legend in matplotlib, things can get dirty. Legendkit may solve your headache. ## Features - Easy title placement and alignment - Easy colorbar with shape - Layout for multiple legends and colorbar* ## Installation ```shell pip install legendkit ```

classes.name: legendkit._colorart.ColorArt

methods.name: legendkit._colorart.ColorArt._get_text_size
documentation: Used to get the proper size for drawing area

methods.name: legendkit._colorart.ColorArt._get_locator_formatter
documentation: Determine the locator to get ticks

methods.name: legendkit._colorart.ColorArt._extend_lower
documentation: Return whether the lower limit is open ended.

methods.name: legendkit._colorart.ColorArt._extend_upper
documentation: Return whether the upper limit is open ended.

Axes-independent colorbar

Most of the time, it's safe to use {func}`colorart`
as a drop-in replacement for {func}`colorbar`.

# Parameters

mappable

: The mappable whose colormap and norm will be used.

norm

: The normalization to use.

cmap

: The colormap to use.

ax

: The axes to draw colorbar.

alpha

: Control the transparency

values :
boundaries :
flip : bool

> Flip the

orientation

: The orientation of colorart.

ticks : list of ticks or Locator
format : str or Formatter
tick_width : float

> The width of tick.

tick_size

: The length of tick.

tick_color

: The color of tick.

ticklocation

: The location of the colorbar ticks.

width

: The width of colorart

height

: The height of colorart

borderpad

: The fractional whitespace inside the colorart border,
  in font-size units.

textpad

: The space between label and the colorart

borderaxespad

: The pad between the axes and colorart border, in font-size units.

prop

: The font properties of the colorart.

fontsize

: The fontsize

title

: The title of colorart

title_fontsize

: The fontsize of title

title_fontproperties

: The fontproperties of title

alignment

: The alignment of the colorart and title.

loc

: Apart from the default location code, you can add 'out' as prefix
  to place the legend ouside the axes.
  See {ref}`all available options. <tutorial/title&layout:Legend Placement>`

deviation

: The space between colorbar and axes if place outside

bbox_to_anchor
bbox_transform
rasterized : bool

> Whether to rasterize the colorart,
> reduce file size in vectorized backend.

# Examples

```
.. plot::

    >>> from legendkit import colorart
    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 10)
    >>> mp = plt.pcolormesh(data, cmap="RdBu")
    >>> colorart(mp)
```

modules.name: legendkit
documentation: Legend creation and manipulation with ease for matplotlib

functions.name: legendkit.layout.stack
documentation:
Stack multiple artists together

# Parameters

legends : list of legends or artists
ax : The axes to draw upon
orientation : {'vertical', 'horizontal'}
spacing : float

> The space between legends

padding

: The space around the legends

align : str
mode
loc
frameon
bbox_to_anchor
bbox_transform
deviation : float

> The space that deviate from axes

title

: The text of title

title_loc

: The location of title

titlepad

: The space between title and legend entries

alignment

: The alignment of the elements inside box

title_fontproperties

: The font dict that configurate title

# Examples

Horizontal stack

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from legendkit import cat_legend, hstack
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(3, 1)); ax.set_axis_off()
    >>> args = dict(colors = ["#A7D2CB", "#F2D388"],
    ...             labels = ["Item 1", "Item 2"])
    >>> legs = [cat_legend(**args, title=f"Legend {i+1}") for i in range(3)]
    >>> hstack(legs, title="Horizontal Stack", loc="center", spacing=10, ax=ax)
```

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from legendkit import cat_legend, vstack
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(3, 3)); ax.set_axis_off()
    >>> args = dict(colors = ["#A7D2CB", "#F2D388"],
    ...             labels = ["Item 1", "Item 2"])
    >>> legs = [cat_legend(**args, title=f"Legend {i+1}") for i in range(3)]
    >>> vstack(legs, title="Vertical Stack", loc="center", spacing=10, ax=ax)
```

classes.name: legendkit._colorbar.Colorbar
documentation:
Colorbar based on Axes

For other parameters, see `colorbar <matplotlib.color.Colorbar>`

# Parameters

mappable

: The mappable whose colormap and norm will be used.

norm

: The normalization to use.

cmap

: The colormap to use.

ax

: The axes to draw colorbar.

style : {'white', 'normal'}, default: 'white'
width : float

> The width of colorbar

height

: The height of colorbar

loc

: Apart from the default location code, you can add 'out' as prefix
  to place the legend ouside the axes.
  See {ref}`all available options. <tutorial/title&layout:Legend Placement>`

deviation

: The space between colorbar and axes if place outside

bbox_to_anchor
bbox_transform
axes_class
axes_kwargs
borderpad
orientation : {'vertical', 'horizontal'}

> The orientation of the colorbar

title

: The title of colorbar

alignment

: The alignment of title and colorbar

title_fontproperties
colorbar_options : mapping

> Pass to {class}`matplotlib.colorbar.Colorbar`

# Examples

```
.. plot::

    >>> from legendkit import colorbar
    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 10)
    >>> mp = plt.pcolormesh(data, cmap="RdBu")
    >>> colorbar(mp)
```

modules.name: legendkit.handles
documentation:
# Reusable Hanldes for Legend

## Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from legendkit import legend
    >>> from legendkit.handles import SquareItem, CircleItem, RectItem, LineItem, BoxplotItem
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(1, 1.5)); ax.set_axis_off()
    >>> legend(ax, handles=[SquareItem(), CircleItem(),
    ...                     RectItem(), LineItem(), BoxplotItem()],
    ...        labels=['Square', 'Circle', 'Rect', 'Line', 'Boxplot'])
```

documentation: Ensure render as square

classes.name: legendkit.handles.RectItem
documentation: Ensure render as rectangle

classes.name: legendkit.handles.CircleItem

classes.name: legendkit._legend.ListLegend
documentation:
This is a modified legend based the original matplotlib class

List of sementic legend handle to be used:
: - rect
  - circle
  - square
  - line
  - boxplot
  - triangle (triangle-up, triangle-down, triangle-left, triangle-right)
  - diamond
  - octagon
  - pentagon
  - hexagon
  - star
  - plus
  - cross
  - asterisk
  - The markers in {mod}`matplotlib.markers`

# Parameters

ax

: The axes to draw the legend

legend_items

: See examples

handles

: A list of legend handler

labels

: A list of legend labels

title_loc

: The location of title

alignment

: How to align the whole legendbox,
  if title is placed on top or bottom, default is 'left';
  if title is placed on left and right, default is 'center';

titlepad

: The space between title and legend entries

draw

: Whether to draw the legend

handler_map : dict
loc : str

> Apart from the default location code, you can add 'out' as prefix
> to place the legend ouside the axes.
> See {ref}`all available options. <tutorial/title&layout:Legend Placement>`

deviation

: The space between legend and axes if legend is placed ouside axes.

frameon

: Draw a frame around legend. Legendkit will not show frame by default

kwargs :

: For other paramters, please see {class}`Legend <matplotlib.legend.Legend>`

# Examples

Create legend from existing axes

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from legendkit import legend
    >>> x = np.arange(0, 10, 0.1)
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> ax.plot(x, 2*x + 1, label="Line1")
    >>> ax.plot(x, 5*x + 1, label="Line2")
    >>> legend(ax, title="Title", alignment="left")

```

Create legend semantically

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(1, 1.5))
    >>> ax.set_axis_off()
    >>> legend(ax, legend_items=[
    ...     # (handle, label, styles)
    ...     ('square', 'Item 1', {'color': '#01949A'}),
    ...     ('circle', 'Item 2', {'facecolor': '#004369',
    ...                           'edgecolor': '#DB1F48'}),
    ...     ('rect', 'Item 3', {'color': '#E5DDC8'}),
    ...     # Or you can have no config at all
    ...     ('line', 'Item 4'),
    ...     ('boxplot', 'Item 5'),
    ... ])
```

classes.name: legendkit._legend.CatLegend
documentation:
Categorical legend with same handles

This is useful to create legend that share
the same handle but with different colors

# Parameters

ax

: The axes to draw the legend

colors

: The color for each legend item

labels

: The text for each legend item

handle

: The handle to be used for every entry, see {class}`legendkit.legend`

handler_kw

: Use this to control the style of handler

fill

: If not filled, the color will draw on the edge.

size

: The size of legend handle

kwargs :

: Pass to {func}`legendkit.legend`

# Examples

Create a legend easily

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from legendkit import cat_legend
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(1, 1))
    >>> ax.set_axis_off()
    >>> cat_legend(ax,
    ...            colors=["red", "blue", "green"],
    ...            labels=["Item 1", "Item 2", "Item 3"],
    ...            )
```
TODO: handle should support path

classes.name: legendkit._legend.SizeLegend
documentation:
Create legend that represent the size of circle

# Parameters

sizes

: The sizes array of all circles on the plot, the unit is point\*\*2,
  same as {meth}`scatter <matplotlib.axes.Axes.scatter>`.

ax

: The axes to draw the legend

labels

: The labels of the legend

array

: The actual data used in plotting, will be used to
  display labels if labels are not specific

colors

: The color of the entry

fmt

: The format or formatter to use for the labels.

func

: A function to calculate the labels.

show_at

: The percentile to show the sizes

spacing

: The spacing of the sizes

handle

: You can use any markers in :module:matplotlib.markers

handler_kw

: Use this to control the style of handler

fill

: If not filled, the color will draw on the edge.

kwargs

: Pass to {func}`legendkit.legend`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from legendkit import size_legend
    >>> sizes = np.arange(0, 101, 10)
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(1, 1.5)); ax.set_axis_off()
    >>> size_legend(sizes)
```

Change the looking of legends

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> array = sizes * 10
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(1, 1.5)); ax.set_axis_off()
    >>> size_legend(sizes, array=array,
    ...             show_at=[.2, .4, .6, .8, 1.],
    ...             colors=['r', 'r', '.5', '.5', 'g'],
    ...             handler_kw=dict(ec='orange'))
```
Modified from mpl.collections.PathCollection.legend_elements
